title: The Rise, Fall, and Rise (and Fall...?) of Cell and Gene Therapy - TimmermanReport.com
gist: DeciBio partner Carl Schoellhammer argues that in vivo cell therapy — the industry's newest hot theme after $14B+ in acquisitions in 18 months — is repeating the hype cycle of the first CAR-T/AAV boom, with the same overcrowded-indication dynamics, a shrinking pool of strategic acquirers, and manufacturing complexity merely shifted (not eliminated) into LNP conjugation and CDMO bottlenecks.

themes.theme: In vivo cell therapy: the new hype wave

insights.claim: More than $14 billion in announced acquisition value flowed into in vivo cell therapy companies over the 18 months prior to mid-2026, including Lilly's acquisition of Kelonia, AbbVie's purchase of Capstan, BMS's acquisition of Orbital, and AstraZeneca's purchase of EsoBiotec.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: A concrete, named list of recent large-pharma M&A targets in in vivo cell therapy — useful comp set for valuing the next wave of private companies in the space and for tracking which big pharmas have already committed capital.
insights.quote: More than $14 billion in announced acquisition value has flowed into in vivo cell therapy companies over the last 18 months. Lilly acquired Kelonia, AbbVie bought Capstan, BMS acquired Orbital, and AstraZeneca bought EsoBiotec, to name a few.

insights.claim: In vivo cell therapy aims to reprogram cells directly inside the body via a single injection, eliminating the complex shipping logistics, waiting times, and toxic preconditioning regimens required by first-generation ex vivo cell therapies (which extract, engineer, and reinfuse a patient's own cells).
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: Frames the core value proposition investors are underwriting: if in vivo delivery works, it removes the biggest commercial-scaling bottleneck of ex vivo CAR-T (patient-specific manufacturing logistics) — the thesis to stress-test before backing any in vivo platform company.
insights.quote: These acquisitions demonstrate that we're getting closer to the day when single injections that reprogram cells can fight disease without complex shipping logistics, waiting times and toxic preconditioning regimens of first-generation ex vivo cell therapies.

insights.claim: In vivo cell therapy is generating investor excitement that explicitly echoes the talking points of the first cell-and-gene-therapy boom: promises that manufacturing will be simplified, costs will fall, and platform technologies will unlock dozens of indications.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: An explicit pattern-recognition warning from an industry insider (DeciBio) that current in vivo cell therapy pitches are structurally identical to the pitches that preceded the 2021-2022 cell/gene therapy bust — a reason to discount platform-hype narratives and demand differentiated clinical data.
insights.quote: Many of today's talking points from companies echo the promises made during the first cell and gene therapy boom. Manufacturing will be simplified, costs will fall, and platform technologies will unlock dozens of indications.

themes.theme: Lessons from the first CAR-T/AAV boom-bust cycle

insights.claim: During the pandemic-era biotech boom, capital flooded into cell and gene therapy in search of 'the next Moderna' — a platform technology with broad potential across multiple diseases — and, per DeciBio's TheraTrack database, the average disease indication attracted more than four gene therapy assets and more than ten cell therapy assets in development simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: A quantified measure of overcrowding (4+ gene therapy assets, 10+ cell therapy assets per indication) that investors can use as a base-rate warning sign for any hot biotech subsector — check current asset density per indication before assuming a company has a differentiated position.
insights.quote: there was a period where the average indication being addressed... attracted more than four gene therapy assets and more than 10 cell therapy assets in development. Multiple companies chased the same diseases with limited differentiation.

insights.claim: During the first cell and gene therapy boom, capital poured into the sector faster than market realities could justify, and scientific possibility increasingly became confused with commercial inevitability — an explicit causal account of why the subsequent correction occurred.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: A reusable diagnostic for spotting late-stage biotech bubbles: watch for the rhetorical slippage from 'this is scientifically possible' to 'this is commercially inevitable' as a tell that capital allocation has outrun fundamentals.
insights.quote: Capital poured in faster than market realities could justify, and scientific possibility increasingly became confused with commercial inevitability.

insights.claim: The first cell and gene therapy wave delivered effective, generally durable treatments rather than the one-and-done 'cures' the market had been promised, and the resulting market correction, while severe, is characterized as having been necessary and ultimately beneficial in forcing companies to improve execution.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: Reframes the 2022 cell/gene therapy crash not as a failure of the science but as a correction of over-promised commercial framing — useful priors for evaluating whether current in vivo hype is making the same 'cure' overclaim versus setting realistic expectations.
insights.quote: The sector delivered effective, generally durable treatments, not the one-and-done "cures" the market was promised. The resulting correction was severe, but necessary... The correction hurt. It also forced companies to step up their game.

insights.claim: Despite being written off by some investors as dead in 2022, the cell and gene therapy sector has since seen several approved products reach blockbuster status and has continued to attract smaller but still meaningful investment and generate significant transaction value.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: Evidence that sectors pronounced 'dead' after a hype-cycle bust can still generate durable commercial winners and ongoing M&A activity — a counter-argument to fully exiting a beaten-down subsector, if underlying products are reaching commercial scale.
insights.quote: Sectors written off by some investors as dead in 2022 have steadily continued to attract smaller, but still meaningful investments and generate significant transaction value.

insights.claim: Manufacturing challenges in cell and gene therapy that once seemed existential (i.e., threatening to the field's viability) are steadily being converted from scientific uncertainties into tractable engineering problems.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: Signals that the 'unsolvable manufacturing problem' framing used to justify sector pessimism in 2022 is outdated — an argument for re-rating legacy CAR-T/AAV manufacturing and CDMO players as the science-to-engineering transition completes.
insights.quote: The manufacturing challenges that once seemed existential are being converted, steadily, from scientific uncertainties into engineering problems.

themes.theme: A shrinking buyer pool is the key overlooked risk

insights.claim: The most logical strategic acquirers for in vivo cell therapy assets have largely already committed via the recent wave of acquisitions, leaving a meaningfully thinner pool of potential buyers for the next cohort of companies now raising capital, building platforms, and running early-stage trials.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: This is the essay's sharpest, most actionable and non-obvious call: exit liquidity for in vivo cell therapy startups is deteriorating even as new capital keeps flowing in — investors should map which pharmas have NOT yet acquired an in vivo asset before assuming an exit path exists.
insights.quote: The most logical strategic buyers have largely committed. That leaves a meaningfully thinner pool of potential acquirers for the companies now raising capital, building platforms, and conducting early-stage trials. Exit paths matter, and the number of natural buyers for the next cohort may be more limited than the current pace of investment implies.
insights.locator: End of opening section, before 'Manufacturing Challenges Ahead'

insights.claim: Investors and companies have flocked into in vivo cell therapy, but the explicit open question the author poses is: given how many strategic buyers have already transacted, who is actually left to acquire the next wave of companies being funded today?
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: Frames a forward-looking supply/demand mismatch in M&A: investment pace in new platform startups is not being matched by a growing acquirer base, implying valuation risk or down-round/failed-exit risk for later entrants.
insights.quote: Investors and companies have flocked to the sector. But who is left to buy the next wave of companies?

themes.theme: Manufacturing complexity is displaced, not eliminated

insights.claim: The common narrative that in vivo cell therapy eliminates manufacturing complexity is incorrect; in many cases the field is simply trading one manufacturing challenge (ex vivo cell processing) for another (targeted LNP production), rather than removing manufacturing difficulty altogether.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: Directly rebuts a key bull-case talking point used to justify current valuations — investors should discount any pitch that claims in vivo approaches are manufacturing-simplicity plays until CDMO/process data proves otherwise.
insights.quote: One of the most common narratives surrounding in vivo cell therapy is that it eliminates manufacturing complexity. It is more complicated than that. In many cases, we may simply be trading one manufacturing challenge for another.

insights.claim: A targeted LNP-based in vivo cell therapy system requires at least three distinct manufacturing steps — mRNA cargo synthesis, lipid nanoparticle formulation/encapsulation, and conjugation of a targeting ligand to the LNP surface — each requiring separate purification and each introducing yield losses that compound across the process.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: A concrete process-engineering breakdown investors can use to interrogate any in vivo LNP company's manufacturing claims — ask specifically about yield loss at each of these three steps, not just headline cost-per-dose figures.
insights.quote: Production involves at least three distinct steps, each requiring purification and each introducing yield losses: synthesis of the mRNA cargo, formulation and encapsulation within the lipid nanoparticle, and conjugation of the targeting ligand to the LNP surface. Losses compound at every stage.

insights.claim: Few organizations possess deep manufacturing expertise across all the disciplines required for targeted LNP products simultaneously (lipid chemistry, biologics manufacturing, nanoparticle formulation, and conjugation science), creating a structural CDMO bottleneck as these programs scale and face increasingly complex regulatory and analytical comparability requirements.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific investable bottleneck: CDMOs with genuine cross-disciplinary capability (lipid + biologics + nanoparticle + conjugation) are scarce and will be gatekeepers as the in vivo cell therapy pipeline scales — a candidate thesis for CDMO/picks-and-shovels investment.
insights.quote: Targeted LNP products sit at the intersection of lipid chemistry, biologics manufacturing, nanoparticle formulation, and conjugation science. While each capability exists independently, relatively few organizations have deep expertise across all of them within a single integrated process.

insights.claim: Conjugating antibodies or peptides to lipid nanoparticle surfaces for targeted delivery raises unresolved technical questions around conjugation efficiency, batch-to-batch consistency, and analytical characterization that the field is still actively working through.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: Flags specific, still-unsolved CMC (chemistry, manufacturing, controls) risk factors for targeted LNP platforms — due diligence checklist items for any in vivo cell therapy investment before assuming manufacturability is de-risked.
insights.quote: Conjugating antibodies or peptides to lipid nanoparticle surfaces introduces questions around conjugation efficiency, batch-to-batch consistency, and analytical characterization that the field is still working through.

themes.theme: Unresolved clinical risk and the sector's meta-lesson

insights.claim: As in vivo cell therapy programs move into larger patient populations and longer follow-up periods, new and currently unanswered questions will emerge around safety, durability, repeat dosing, and immune responses — risks that have not yet been tested at scale.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: Explicitly flags that current in vivo cell therapy valuations are being set before key long-term clinical risks (durability, repeat dosing, immunogenicity) have been resolved — a reason to size positions conservatively until later-phase data emerges.
insights.quote: Clinical reality will also eventually arrive. As programs move into larger patient populations and longer follow-up periods, new questions will emerge around safety, durability, repeat dosing, and immune responses.

insights.claim: The author's overarching meta-lesson is that cell and gene therapy has never been a story of failure but rather a repeating pattern of overestimation followed, more quietly, by real underlying progress — and the present danger is mistaking early promise for inevitability a second time.
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: The essay's closing thesis is a general-purpose warning framework for any hyped biotech modality: expect real long-term value creation, but expect it to arrive after (not instead of) another hype-driven overshoot and correction — a timing signal for when to actually deploy capital.
insights.quote: Cell and gene therapy has never been a story of failure. It has been a story of overestimation followed, more quietly, by real progress. The danger today is that we once again mistake early promise for inevitability.

insights.claim: The author frames the sector's trajectory with an explicit rollercoaster metaphor: if the field applies lessons learned from AAV and CAR-T, the next decade could be transformative, but if not, the industry risks 'boarding the next rollercoaster before the last one has finished its climb.'
insights.speaker: Carl Schoellhammer
insights.so_what: A binary framing investors can use to bucket their own thesis: is this specific in vivo company demonstrably applying AAV/CAR-T-era lessons (disciplined indication selection, realistic manufacturing claims, patient buyer pool) or repeating the same mistakes?
insights.quote: If the field applies the lessons learned from AAV and CAR-T, the next decade could be transformative. If not, we may simply be boarding the next rollercoaster before the last one has finished its climb.
